reasoning: The vulnerability is reachable at default configuration with no non-default options required. The Form constructor sets autoFiles=false by default, but lines 82-84 show that simply adding a 'file' event listener (self.on('newListener', ...)) automatically flips autoFiles=true. Likewise, passing a callback to parse() sets both autoFiles=true and autoFields=true (lines 98-101). These are the two most common usage patterns documented in the library README. Once autoFiles is true, handleFile() is called for every uploaded file part, and publicFile.originalFilename is set directly from fileStream.filename (line 701), which is the raw value returned by parseFilename(). parseFilename() at line 853 only strips Windows-style backslash path components via lastIndexOf('\\'), but does NOT strip forward-slash sequences such as '../../../etc/passwd'. The value is then emitted to all 'file' event listeners at line 738 as publicFile.originalFilename. No schema validation, allowlist, or sanitization exists in the parsing pipeline. The actual on-disk upload path (uploadPath, line 831-835) is safe because it uses uid.sync(18) as the base filename and only appends the FILE_EXT_RE-sanitized extension, so the library itself does not directly write to attacker-controlled paths. However, the dangerous sink is originalFilename being exposed to application code: if any downstream consumer (express route, custom handler, framework integration) passes publicFile.originalFilename to fs.rename, fs.createWriteStream, path.join, or similar filesystem operations without sanitization, path traversal results. This is a well-known dangerous pattern documented in CVE-2021-43803 (Next.js used req.file.originalFilename directly). Any unauthenticated HTTP client sending a multipart/form-data request with Content-Disposition filename containing '../' sequences can exploit downstream consumers. Severity remains medium because exploitation requires the downstream application to misuse originalFilename — the library itself does not directly write to the traversed path.
